title: Git Workflows and CI/CD
learningObjectives: Compare GitFlow, GitHub Flow, and trunk-based development and choose appropriately
Write conventional commits following the Conventional Commits specification
Use semantic-release for automated versioning and CHANGELOG generation
Sign commits with GPG or SSH keys for verified authorship
Integrate Git with GitHub Actions for automated CI/CD pipelines
Use git maintenance for background repository optimisation on large repos

theory.content:
Git Workflow Strategies

Choosing the right branching strategy has a significant impact on team velocity, release cadence, and merge conflict frequency. The three most common strategies are:

WorkflowBranchesRelease modelBest for
GitFlowmain + develop + feature/* + release/* + hotfix/*Versioned, scheduled releasesLarge teams, open-source with version numbers
GitHub Flowmain + short-lived feature branchesContinuous deployment on mergeSmall teams, SaaS apps
Trunk-Based Developmentmain only; feature branches live less than 1 dayContinuous + feature flagsHigh-velocity teams, mature DevOps culture

Recommendation: trunk-based development with feature flags is the fastest path to CI/CD because every merge to main triggers a deploy, and feature flags decouple deployment from release. GitFlow is a better fit for open-source projects that ship numbered releases like v2.3.1 and need a develop buffer before stabilising.

Conventional Commits

The Conventional Commits specification defines a structured commit message format that is both human-readable and machine-parseable. The basic format is:

type(scope): description

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]

Common types and their semantic version effect:

  • feat — new feature → minor version bump (1.2.0 → 1.3.0)
  • fix — bug fix → patch version bump (1.2.0 → 1.2.1)
  • docs, chore, refactor, test, style, perf, ci — no version bump by default
  • feat! or any type with a BREAKING CHANGE: footer → major version bump (1.2.0 → 2.0.0)

Example commit with all fields:

feat(api)!: remove deprecated /v1/users endpoint

The /v1/users endpoint introduced in v0.9 has been removed.
All integrations must migrate to /v2/users which supports
pagination and filtering.

BREAKING CHANGE: The /v1/users endpoint no longer exists.
Closes #142

Semantic Release

semantic-release automates the entire release workflow driven by conventional commit messages. Running npx semantic-release in CI will:

  1. Read commit history since the last Git tag
  2. Determine the next semantic version based on commit types
  3. Create and push a new Git tag
  4. Generate and commit an updated CHANGELOG.md
  5. Publish the package (npm, GitHub Releases, etc.) with release notes

Requires: a CI environment, a .releaserc or semantic-release config in package.json, and all commits since last release to follow the Conventional Commits spec. A single feat! commit in the set triggers a major version bump regardless of other commits.

Signed Commits

Signed commits prove that a commit was authored by someone who controls a specific cryptographic key. GitHub and GitLab display a green Verified badge on signed commits.

GPG signing (traditional):

# Generate a key
gpg --gen-key

# List keys to find your KEY_ID
gpg --list-secret-keys --keyid-format LONG

# Configure Git to use your key
git config --global user.signingkey KEY_ID
git config --global commit.gpgsign true

# Sign a single commit manually
git commit -S -m "feat: add login"

# Verify signatures in the log
git log --show-signature

SSH signing (simpler, Git 2.34+):

# Use your existing SSH key — no gpg binary needed
git config --global gpg.format ssh
git config --global user.signingkey ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
git config --global commit.gpgsign true

# Add your public key to GitHub: Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New signing key

SSH signing is increasingly preferred for its simplicity — most developers already have SSH keys configured for push access.

GitHub Actions Integration

GitHub Actions workflows live in .github/workflows/*.yml and are triggered by Git events. A minimal CI workflow for a Node.js project:

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test

Key integration patterns:

  • Protected branches: require a green status check (this workflow) before a PR can merge to main
  • Git identity in CI: git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com" before committing generated files
  • Semantic release in CI: add a release job that runs npx semantic-release after tests pass on main

git maintenance

Introduced in Git 2.29 and significantly improved in 2.47+, git maintenance runs optimisation tasks in the background on a system timer (systemd on Linux, launchd on macOS, Task Scheduler on Windows), so you never wait for gc to finish during interactive use.

# Register the repo for background maintenance
git maintenance start

# Run a specific task on demand
git maintenance run --task=prefetch
git maintenance run --task=gc
git maintenance run --task=commit-graph

# Stop the background timer
git maintenance stop

# List registered repos
git maintenance list

Available tasks:

  • prefetch — keeps remote-tracking refs fresh in the background
  • gc — garbage collection and packfile consolidation
  • commit-graph — writes a .git/objects/info/commit-graph file that speeds up git log, git merge-base, and reachability checks by 10-100x on large repos
  • loose-objects — packs loose objects without full gc
  • incremental-repack — repacks in small increments to avoid long pauses
